summary: Ojibwe American novelist and poet. Best known for Love Medicine (1984), an episodic novel set on a Chippewa (Ojibwe) reservation in North Dakota, following interlinked families including the Kashpaws and Lazarres. Also wrote The Round House, The Night Watchman (2020 Pulitzer), The Painted Drum, The Sentence, and Future Home of the Living God. Owns Birchbark Books in Minneapolis. Her sister Heid E. Erdrich is also a poet.

works.name: Love Medicine
works.description: Erdrich's debut novel (1984), an episodic work set on a Chippewa (Ojibwe) reservation in North Dakota. It opens with June Kashpaw (or June Morrissey) dying as she walks home in a snowstorm, freezing to death. Key characters include the love triangle between Marie Lazarre, Lulu Lamartine, and Nector Kashpaw — Marie marries Nector despite his assault of her. Lipsha plans to use goose hearts to make the title 'love medicine' to cement Marie and Nector's relationship, since geese mate for life. The first chapter was originally a short story called 'The World's Greatest Fisherman.' Stories like 'The Red Convertible' and 'Saint Marie' were incorporated into the novel. In 'Saint Marie,' Marie Lazarre has scalding water poured in her ear by a Satan-obsessed nun (Sister Leopoldina).

works.name: "The Red Convertible"
works.description: A story about brothers Lyman and Henry. After Henry returns from the Vietnam War deeply changed, he jumps into a river and tells Lyman 'my boots are filling' before drowning. Lyman then drives the title red convertible into the river after his brother's death. The story was incorporated into Love Medicine.

examples: Henry jumps into a river and tells his brother Lyman 'my boots are filling' before he drowns
the narrator drives the title vehicle into a river after his brother's boots fill up with water

works.name: The Round House
works.description: Part of Erdrich's 'Justice Trilogy' about sexual violence on reservations. Joe Coutts investigates the assault of his mother Geraldine by Linden Lark at the round house. His friend Cappy aids Joe's attempt at revenge.

works.name: The Night Watchman
works.description: A 2020 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel inspired by Erdrich's grandfather's resistance to Native termination policies. Set around a factory in the Turtle Mountain Indian Reservation. Thomas Wazhashk leaves a cryptic message about an ominous owl at his station. Features Pixie Parenteau swimming in a costume of Babe the Blue Ox at a nightclub.

works.name: The Painted Drum
works.description: A novel in which Bernard narrates the multigenerational history of an instrument discovered by Faye Travers. In the opening, a sculptor's daughter dies when her boyfriend drives their car off a bridge. An unfaithful wife sacrifices her daughter to wolves while traveling over a frozen lake, and the child's bones are placed within the title object.

works.name: The Sentence
works.description: A novel in which Tookie is incarcerated for nine years. It is partially set in a Minneapolis bookshop owned by Erdrich (Birchbark Books). The protagonist attempts to exorcise a ghost by reading Proust to her. A woman transports a dead body in a stolen refrigerated truck, unaware it has cocaine taped to its armpits.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Erdrich also wrote Future Home of the Living God (2017), a dystopian novel in which Cedar writes a letter to her unborn child under a 'gravid female detention' policy. She co-wrote The Crown of Columbus with her husband Michael Dorris, about two academics recovering Christopher Columbus's crown. A character poses for a nude painting hung in the state capitol building (from The Plague of Doves). Erdrich studied creative writing under John Barth.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Louise Erdrich (Karen Louise Erdrich) is an Ojibwe (Chippewa) American author who is half-German-American. She owns Birchbark Books, a bookshop in Minneapolis that supports writing workshops to preserve Native American writing. Her sister Heid E. Erdrich is a poet who wrote the cookbook Original Local and the poem 'The Theft Outright' (which inverts Robert Frost's 'The Gift Outright'). Erdrich's work is centrally concerned with Native American reservation life, particularly in North Dakota.

comprehensive_summary:
Louise Erdrich (Karen Louise Erdrich) is an Ojibwe (Chippewa) American novelist who is one of the most prominent Native American authors in quizbowl. She owns Birchbark Books, a bookshop in Minneapolis, and is half-German-American, half-Chippewa. Her sister Heid E. Erdrich is a poet who wrote the cookbook Original Local and the poem 'The Theft Outright' (which inverts Robert Frost's 'The Gift Outright').

Her debut and most frequently clued novel is Love Medicine (1984), an episodic work set on a Chippewa reservation in North Dakota. It opens with June Kashpaw freezing to death while walking home in a snowstorm, which is the single most common clue about Erdrich. The novel follows interlinked families: Marie Lazarre marries Nector Kashpaw despite his assault of her, forming a love triangle with Lulu Lamartine. Lipsha tries to use goose hearts (since geese mate for life) to make the title 'love medicine' for Marie and Nector. Stories like 'The Red Convertible' (about brothers Henry and Lyman — Henry drowns after Vietnam, telling Lyman 'my boots are filling') and 'Saint Marie' (scalding water poured in Marie's ear by a nun) were incorporated into the novel.

The Round House is part of Erdrich's 'Justice Trilogy' about sexual violence on reservations, following Joe Coutts as he investigates the assault of his mother Geraldine by Linden Lark, aided by his friend Cappy. The Night Watchman (2020 Pulitzer) is inspired by Erdrich's grandfather's resistance to Native termination policies at the Turtle Mountain Indian Reservation. The Painted Drum features an unfaithful wife who sacrifices her daughter to wolves on a frozen lake. The Sentence features Tookie's incarceration and a ghost exorcised by reading Proust. Erdrich also co-wrote The Crown of Columbus with her husband Michael Dorris and studied creative writing under John Barth.

Short Story: after returning from Vietnam, Henry jumps into a river and tells his brother Lyman 'my boots are filling' before drowning; Lyman drives the title vehicle into the river
Novel: Joe Coutts investigates the assault of his mother Geraldine by Linden Lark; part of a 'Justice Trilogy' about sexual violence on reservations
Novel: 2020 Pulitzer winner inspired by the author's grandfather's resistance to Native termination policies at Turtle Mountain
Novel: an unfaithful wife sacrifices her daughter to wolves while traveling over a frozen lake; the bones are placed in the title object
Novel: in 'Saint Marie,' a Satan-obsessed nun pours scalding water into the title character's ear
